clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Hepatocellular carcinoma is a primary liver malignancy that often arises in chronically injured liver tissue and may present with subtle constitutional or abdominal symptoms.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Malignant transformation of hepatocytes within a chronically damaged liver creates a focal tumor whose behavior reflects both cancer biology and liver reserve.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often evolves over months to years on top of chronic liver disease
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may notice fatigue, abdominal discomfort, weight loss, or decompensation clues rather than a single dramatic early symptom.

physiologyPrerequisites.topic: basic liver structure and blood supply
physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to understand why local vascular behavior and organ reserve matter to the story.
The detective work depends on understanding why a tumor in the liver affects markers, symptoms, and treatment choice.

pathophysiology.event: chronic liver injury creates a pro-carcinogenic environment
pathophysiology.mechanism: Inflammation, regeneration, and genomic injury increase malignant transformation risk.

pathophysiology.event: a malignant hepatocyte clone expands into a focal tumor
pathophysiology.mechanism: Autonomous growth distorts normal architecture and competes with surrounding tissue.

pathophysiology.event: tumor growth alters local vascular behavior and hepatic reserve
pathophysiology.mechanism: Expansion changes perfusion and contributes to liver dysfunction or mass-effect findings.

commonSigns: jaundice when liver dysfunction is present
historyClues: background chronic liver disease may be present
physicalExamClues: liver edge fullness can coexist with chronic liver disease stigmata
presentation.typicalTimecourse: often insidious until surveillance or progressive symptoms reveal the lesion

labs.name: liver panel
labs.purpose: characterize hepatic function and injury pattern
labs.expectedFinding: may show injury or dysfunction but is not diagnostic by itself

labs.name: alpha-fetoprotein
labs.purpose: supportive tumor marker when elevated in the right context
labs.expectedFinding: may be elevated in some cases and should be treated as a clue rather than a universal rule

imaging.name: multiphasic liver imaging
imaging.purpose: characterize lesion behavior and vascular pattern
imaging.expectedFinding: arterial enhancement with washout may support diagnosis in context

pathology.name: tumor histology
pathology.expectedFinding: malignant hepatocellular features when tissue is required

diagnosticLogic: Interpret the lesion in the setting of chronic liver disease risk.
Use imaging behavior, clinical context, and selected biomarkers together.
Avoid treating one biomarker as a stand-alone diagnosis.

differentials.whyConsidered: another primary liver malignancy can produce focal lesions and systemic symptoms
differentials.whyLessLikely: pattern of imaging behavior and hepatocellular biology can point away from biliary-origin malignancy

differentials.whyConsidered: liver lesions may reflect spread from an extrahepatic cancer
differentials.whyLessLikely: clinical context and lesion behavior may support a primary hepatocellular tumor instead

acuteStabilization: stabilize bleeding, pain, or decompensation when present
address urgent complications of liver dysfunction as clinically indicated

definitiveTherapies.name: surgical or locoregional therapy
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: remove or destroy tumor tissue in selected localized disease contexts
definitiveTherapies.whenUsed: selected early or localized disease with adequate liver reserve

definitiveTherapies.name: systemic therapy
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: target tumor survival pathways or tumor-associated angiogenesis depending on regimen

monitoring: follow liver function and treatment response
notes: management is both stage-aware and liver-function-aware

evidence.claimText: Chronic liver injury increases HCC risk.
HCC reflects malignant transformation of hepatocytes.
Tumor growth changes local perfusion and tissue architecture.
Liver tests may be abnormal in HCC but are not diagnostic by themselves.
AFP may be elevated in HCC but is not universally elevated.
Multiphasic imaging behavior helps identify HCC in the right clinical context.
Histology can confirm malignant hepatocellular features when tissue is needed.
Localized HCC may be treated with surgery or locoregional therapy when clinically appropriate.
Advanced unresectable HCC may require systemic therapy tailored to disease context.

governanceNotes: Preferred baseline source for hepatology and liver tumor evaluation.
Use for imaging behavior and lesion characterization claims.
Use for pathology confirmation and histology language.
Preferred for stage-aware oncology treatment recommendations.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder of the neuromuscular junction that causes fluctuating fatigable weakness.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Autoantibodies impair neuromuscular transmission, so repeated muscle use reveals the weakness more than fixed structural damage does.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often evolves over weeks to months with fluctuating severity
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often notice eyelid drooping, double vision, chewing fatigue, or limb weakness that worsens with use and improves with rest.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story depends on showing transmission failure rather than muscle destruction.

pathophysiology.mechanism: Antibody-mediated disruption reduces efficient signal transmission from nerve to muscle.
Signal safety margin falls with exertion, leading to fatigable weakness.

historyClues: symptoms worsen with use and later in the day
physicalExamClues: strength may decline with repeated effort testing
presentation.typicalTimecourse: fluctuating weakness with exertional worsening is characteristic

imaging.name: chest imaging for thymic disease
imaging.expectedFinding: may identify thymic hyperplasia or thymoma

pathology.name: electrodiagnostic correlation
pathology.expectedFinding: supports transmission disorder rather than primary muscle destruction

diagnosticLogic: Look for fluctuating fatigable weakness rather than fixed sensory loss.
Use antibody and electrodiagnostic data to confirm impaired transmission.

differentials.whyConsidered: also causes weakness through impaired neuromuscular signaling
differentials.whyLessLikely: clinical pattern and antibody findings can point toward MG instead

acuteStabilization: assess respiratory and bulbar status when symptoms are severe

definitiveTherapies.name: symptomatic acetylcholinesterase inhibition
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: improves acetylcholine availability at the neuromuscular junction

definitiveTherapies.name: immunomodulatory therapy
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: reduces autoimmune attack on neuromuscular junction targets
definitiveTherapies.whenUsed: persistent or generalized disease depending on severity and context

monitoring: follow fluctuating weakness, swallowing, and respiratory symptoms
notes: treatment should reflect mechanism-driven transmission failure

evidence.claimText: MG commonly reflects autoantibody-mediated neuromuscular junction dysfunction.
Fatigable weakness is a core clinical pattern in MG.
Acetylcholine receptor antibodies can support the diagnosis of MG.
Chest imaging can assess for thymic disease associated with MG.
Electrodiagnostic studies can support impaired neuromuscular transmission.
Acetylcholinesterase inhibition can provide symptomatic improvement in MG.
Immunomodulatory therapy is used to reduce autoimmune activity in generalized MG.

governanceNotes: Preferred source for neuromuscular junction disorders.
Preferred source for neurologic diagnostic framing.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Community-acquired pneumonia is an infectious process involving the lung parenchyma that commonly causes cough, fever, and dyspnea.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Infection and inflammation fill alveolar spaces, impairing gas exchange and driving respiratory symptoms.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often evolves over days with systemic and respiratory complaints
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients commonly notice cough, fever, shortness of breath, pleuritic pain, or fatigue as the lungs become inflamed.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to see why inflammatory filling of alveoli causes dyspnea and oxygenation problems.

pathophysiology.event: pathogens seed the lower respiratory tract
pathophysiology.mechanism: Microbial invasion triggers a local inflammatory response.
Exudate and cellular debris impair ventilation and gas exchange.

physicalExamClues: focal lung findings can localize the process

labs.name: basic infectious and inflammatory labs
labs.expectedFinding: may show inflammatory response but should be interpreted with the clinical picture

imaging.name: chest radiography
imaging.expectedFinding: infiltrate or consolidation consistent with pneumonia

pathology.name: microbiologic correlation when needed
pathology.expectedFinding: helps identify the infectious cause in selected cases

diagnosticLogic: Combine respiratory symptoms, physical findings, and imaging.
Consider severity and host factors when selecting diagnostics and therapy.

differentials.whyConsidered: shortness of breath and imaging abnormalities can overlap
differentials.whyLessLikely: infectious symptoms and focal inflammatory findings support pneumonia instead

acuteStabilization: support oxygenation and hemodynamics according to severity

definitiveTherapies.name: severity-aware antimicrobial therapy
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: targets the infectious cause while source control comes from immune recovery and ventilation support
definitiveTherapies.whenUsed: after clinical assessment of likely pathogens and severity

monitoring: follow respiratory status and response to treatment
notes: treatment should acknowledge severity context and suspected cause

evidence.claimText: CAP is an infectious process involving lung parenchyma.
Alveolar inflammation impairs gas exchange in pneumonia.
Laboratory findings should be interpreted in clinical context and are not sufficient alone.
Chest imaging supports the diagnosis of pneumonia when correlated with clinical findings.
Microbiologic testing is selected based on severity and clinical need.
Antimicrobial treatment for CAP should reflect severity and likely pathogen coverage.

governanceNotes: Preferred source for pneumonia diagnosis and treatment.

clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Use alveolar infection to orient the detectives before the final diagnosis reveal.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Pulmonary embolism is obstruction of the pulmonary arterial circulation by thromboembolic material that can impair gas exchange and strain the right ventricle.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: A venous clot travels to the pulmonary circulation, where it blocks blood flow, increases dead-space ventilation, and can raise right-sided cardiac afterload.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often presents abruptly over minutes to hours
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may suddenly develop dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, lightheadedness, or unexplained hypoxemia.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to understand why blocked perfusion causes dyspnea even when the airways are open.
The severity arc depends on explaining why a large embolus can destabilize the heart as well as the lungs.

pathophysiology.event: venous thrombosis forms and embolizes to the pulmonary arteries
pathophysiology.mechanism: Clot migration abruptly blocks part of the pulmonary vascular bed.
Dead-space ventilation and hypoxemia emerge because blood flow no longer matches alveolar ventilation.

pathophysiology.event: right-heart strain can develop when clot burden is significant
pathophysiology.mechanism: Acute pulmonary vascular obstruction increases right ventricular afterload and may reduce forward flow.

historyClues: immobility, recent surgery, or prior VTE can raise suspicion
physicalExamClues: respiratory distress can be present despite a relatively clear lung exam
presentation.typicalTimecourse: acute onset over minutes to hours

labs.name: D-dimer in selected low- or intermediate-risk evaluation
labs.purpose: help exclude thromboembolism when pretest probability allows
labs.expectedFinding: abnormality is nonspecific and must be interpreted with pretest probability

imaging.name: CT pulmonary angiography
imaging.purpose: identify embolic obstruction in the pulmonary arteries
imaging.expectedFinding: filling defects consistent with pulmonary emboli when the diagnosis is present

pathology.name: right-heart and severity assessment
pathology.expectedFinding: risk stratification depends on hemodynamic status and evidence of right ventricular strain

diagnosticLogic: Start with pretest probability rather than treating every dyspnea complaint the same way.
Use imaging and severity markers together to separate low-risk from higher-risk embolism.

differentials.whyConsidered: both can present with dyspnea, pleuritic discomfort, and abnormal oxygenation
differentials.whyLessLikely: sudden onset, thrombotic risk factors, and vascular imaging findings may point toward embolism instead

differentials.whyConsidered: chest pain, dyspnea, and instability can overlap
differentials.whyLessLikely: pulmonary vascular imaging or right-heart strain can redirect the workup

acuteStabilization: support oxygenation and perfusion while assessing severity
escalate rapidly when hemodynamic compromise or major right-heart strain is present

definitiveTherapies.name: anticoagulation
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: prevents clot extension and supports endogenous clot resolution
definitiveTherapies.whenUsed: first-line treatment for most confirmed pulmonary emboli when bleeding risk allows

definitiveTherapies.name: reperfusion therapy in selected high-risk cases
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: reduces clot burden when obstructive physiology creates major instability

monitoring: follow oxygenation, hemodynamics, and signs of right-heart strain
reassess bleeding risk and long-term anticoagulation planning
notes: treatment intensity should match embolic risk and hemodynamic impact

evidence.claimText: Pulmonary embolism usually reflects thromboembolic obstruction of the pulmonary arterial circulation.
Pulmonary embolism creates ventilation-perfusion mismatch and can impair oxygenation.
Higher clot burden can cause right ventricular strain and hemodynamic compromise.
D-dimer must be interpreted in the context of pretest probability and is not specific for PE.
CT pulmonary angiography can identify embolic filling defects in the pulmonary arteries.
Risk assessment in PE includes hemodynamics and right ventricular strain assessment.
Anticoagulation is the mainstay of therapy for most confirmed pulmonary emboli.
Selected high-risk pulmonary embolism may require reperfusion therapy.

governanceNotes: Preferred source for venous thromboembolism diagnosis and treatment decisions.
Use for physiologic explanation of gas-exchange and right-heart effects.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Acute pancreatitis is an inflammatory injury of the pancreas caused by premature enzyme activation that can range from localized pain to systemic illness.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Digestive enzyme activation inside the pancreas triggers local autodigestion, inflammation, third spacing, and sometimes multi-organ stress.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: usually evolves over hours to days
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often present with severe epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, and progressive discomfort that can radiate to the back.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The detective story depends on showing why enzymes that should aid digestion become damaging when activated too early.

pathophysiology.event: injury triggers premature pancreatic enzyme activation
pathophysiology.mechanism: Activated enzymes damage pancreatic tissue instead of remaining safely compartmentalized.

pathophysiology.event: local pancreatic inflammation expands into surrounding tissue and the systemic circulation
pathophysiology.mechanism: Inflammation causes edema, pain, fluid shifts, and sometimes organ dysfunction.

commonSigns: volume depletion in more severe cases
historyClues: biliary disease or alcohol exposure may be relevant depending on context
physicalExamClues: significant epigastric tenderness can dominate the exam
presentation.typicalTimecourse: acute inflammatory presentation over hours to days

labs.name: pancreatic enzymes and metabolic labs
labs.purpose: support diagnosis and evaluate inflammatory severity or complications
labs.expectedFinding: elevated pancreatic enzymes support diagnosis in the correct pain syndrome

imaging.name: abdominal imaging when clinically indicated
imaging.purpose: look for gallstones, complications, or alternative diagnoses
imaging.expectedFinding: imaging is guided by severity, uncertainty, and complication concern rather than reflex use in every case

pathology.name: etiology assessment
pathology.expectedFinding: workup should search for common triggers and severity features

diagnosticLogic: Diagnose acute pancreatitis from characteristic pain plus supportive laboratory or imaging evidence.
Do not confuse routine imaging triage with the core diagnosis itself.

differentials.whyConsidered: upper abdominal pain and nausea can overlap substantially
differentials.whyLessLikely: pancreatic enzyme elevation and the broader inflammatory picture can point toward pancreatitis instead

acuteStabilization: provide aggressive early supportive care with fluids, pain control, and monitoring

definitiveTherapies.name: cause-directed management with supportive care
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: limits physiologic stress while the triggering problem and complications are addressed
definitiveTherapies.whenUsed: throughout the acute episode with escalation for complications

monitoring: watch for organ dysfunction, local complications, and failure to improve
notes: supportive care and severity reassessment are central to good outcomes

evidence.claimText: Acute pancreatitis reflects premature pancreatic enzyme activation and inflammatory injury.
Local pancreatic inflammation can create systemic effects and volume shifts.
Diagnosis combines characteristic abdominal pain with supportive pancreatic enzyme elevation or imaging evidence.
Imaging in acute pancreatitis is guided by uncertainty, severity, and complication concern.
Initial evaluation should identify etiology and risk features, including biliary triggers when relevant.
Supportive care with fluids, analgesia, and complication monitoring is central to acute pancreatitis management.

governanceNotes: Preferred source for pancreatitis diagnosis, triage, and supportive care.
Useful for mechanism teaching in gastrointestinal inflammation.
Use for abdominal imaging framing and complication detection.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute metabolic emergency caused by insulin deficiency with ketosis, acidosis, and dehydration.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Insulin deficiency drives unchecked lipolysis and ketone production while osmotic diuresis worsens dehydration and electrolyte disruption.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often evolves over hours to days
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may develop polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, abdominal pain, fatigue, and progressive dehydration before the metabolic pattern becomes obvious.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story depends on showing why lack of insulin triggers both hyperglycemia and ketone production.

pathophysiology.event: insulin deficiency and counterregulatory hormones rise
pathophysiology.mechanism: The body cannot use glucose effectively and shifts toward fat breakdown.

pathophysiology.event: ketone production and osmotic diuresis accelerate
pathophysiology.mechanism: Ketosis causes acidosis while hyperglycemia worsens dehydration and electrolyte loss.

commonSigns: altered mental status in more severe cases
historyClues: progressive insulin deficiency or missed insulin
intercurrent illness may trigger the episode
presentation.typicalTimecourse: rapidly progressive metabolic decompensation over hours to days

labs.name: metabolic panel and ketone assessment
labs.purpose: confirm hyperglycemia, acidosis, and ketosis while monitoring electrolytes
labs.expectedFinding: anion gap metabolic acidosis with ketosis and clinically important electrolyte shifts

pathology.name: trigger evaluation
pathology.expectedFinding: identify precipitating illness or insulin interruption when present

diagnosticLogic: Treat DKA as a combined metabolic and volume/electrolyte emergency.
Confirm ketosis and acidosis rather than reducing the problem to glucose alone.

differentials.whyConsidered: both can present with severe hyperglycemia and dehydration
differentials.whyLessLikely: prominent ketosis and acidosis support DKA instead

acuteStabilization: replace fluids and correct electrolytes while monitoring closely

definitiveTherapies.name: insulin therapy with electrolyte management
definitiveTherapies.mechanismOfAction: turns off ketone production while coordinated electrolyte replacement prevents treatment-related harm
definitiveTherapies.whenUsed: after initial assessment and fluid resuscitation planning

monitoring: track glucose, anion gap, potassium, and clinical recovery
notes: potassium strategy is part of treatment, not an optional add-on

evidence.claimText: Insulin deficiency drives the core metabolic changes in DKA.
Ketosis, acidosis, dehydration, and electrolyte shifts are core features of DKA.
DKA diagnosis depends on confirming ketosis and acidosis alongside hyperglycemia.
Clinicians should evaluate for precipitating illness or insulin interruption in DKA.
Treatment requires fluids, insulin, and careful electrolyte management including potassium strategy.

governanceNotes: Preferred for endocrine emergency stabilization and diagnosis claims.
Useful for mechanistic explanation and teaching context.
